rationales.self_direction: He frames his path as "building something" outside the degree track his family keeps pushing, showing commitment to his own chosen route.
rationales.achievement: He points to concrete performance markers like "closed a deal with two new restaurants this week" and running drivers, which signals active pursuit of success.
rationales.power: He is upset that others still see him as "some kid running errands on a scooter" and wants recognition as someone substantial and in charge.
He keeps circling back to "being the one who fixes everything" and not being able to stand "not being in the middle of it," which shows a strong pull toward control.
rationales.benevolence: He agreed to Sami's request to take Friday off "for a family thing" even though it made his own day harder, showing care toward someone in his orbit.

rationales.achievement: He secured the restaurant account because "my prices were better than the other guy's," showing competitive competence and a concrete business win.
rationales.power: What bothers him most is not just the sale but that he wasn't treated like "the one making decisions," showing strong concern with status and authority.

rationales.hedonism: He turned down football even though "the routes were fine" because he did not want to step away, choosing vigilance over enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in operational success like "all three scooters running," drivers hitting targets, and feeling like he is "running something real."
rationales.power: He likes being the one tracking every route and says he wants to be "right there watching it happen," which reflects a need to stay in control.
rationales.security: "I don't trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention" shows a strong pull toward preserving stability and guarding against disruption.
rationales.benevolence: He rewarded Sami and Yousef with "a small bonus this week," which is a concrete supportive act toward close workers.

rationales.power: He says that in his father's shop he is "just his son moving boxes" and that he "shrink[s] in that space," describing a situation where his sense of influence and standing is undermined.
rationales.tradition: He agrees to help because "Baba asked if I could help in the shop tomorrow because his back is bad again," reflecting family-duty obligations.
rationales.benevolence: Even though he was annoyed, he still said yes to helping his father when his "back is bad again," which is a clear supportive response to a close family member.

rationales.power: The loss hurts because it feels like "what I built wasn't worth staying for," showing that his reaction is tied to whether his enterprise commands loyalty and respect.
rationales.security: He immediately translates the resignation into "losing a rider means losing a route means losing income," framing it as a threat to stability.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects the safe option because "reliable is boring" and prefers the more intense, riskier candidate who is "hungrier."
rationales.achievement: He wants workers who "want to prove something" and keeps talking about people who understand "what we're building," which centers ambition and performance.
rationales.power: He is actively screening, selecting, and mentally planning routes for hires, showing a strong orientation toward directing others and shaping the operation.
rationales.security: He knowingly dismisses the safer choice because "reliable is boring," which cuts against prioritizing predictability and stability.
The no-show forces him to cover "thirteen deliveries in the rain" himself, highlighting disruption, instability, and the cost of unreliable arrangements.

rationales.self_direction: He says he would "rather eat glass" than live like his cousin who asks his father permission before going anywhere, which is an explicit defense of autonomy.
rationales.power: He recognizes that he has been putting himself "at the center of everything" and ties his identity to being "in charge," but the entry is a critical reckoning with that need rather than an endorsement of it.
rationales.tradition: He rejects the conventional path his mother suggests to "find a girl and settle down like my cousin Ahmad," treating that model as intolerable.

rationales.achievement: He reports concrete growth like working with "eleven" restaurants and reflects on a successful first week from the new hire, showing continued business accomplishment.
rationales.power: He notices that "not everything has to go through me for it to count" and that it felt good when Issa handled routes without him, which moves away from needing control.
rationales.security: He values that Issa "showed up every day on time" and managed routes reliably, highlighting appreciation for steadiness and predictability.

rationales.self_direction: She relishes that the owner only asked for "something colorful" because it gives her "so much room to play," emphasizing creative freedom.
rationales.hedonism: She intentionally let herself enjoy the morning, ordering "three cups of chai" and insisting that "I just also let myself have a nice morning."
rationales.achievement: She is actively progressing the commission, noting that "the sketches are coming together" and that she also "finished the commission brief."

rationales.self_direction: She says "I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do" despite repeated pressure, showing firm commitment to her own chosen path.
rationales.conformity: She keeps having to "defend my choices" because others refuse to treat them as valid, which reflects active resistance to social expectations.
rationales.tradition: Her mother's script of "no salary, no benefits, no husband" represents a conventional life script that she is refusing rather than embracing.

rationales.hedonism: After a good day she buys herself "fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not," explicitly rewarding herself with pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: She is teaching a group of kids, celebrates Advait's collage, and is clearly energized by helping children express themselves creatively.

rationales.self_direction: The payoff is that the mural "actually looks like what you imagined," which highlights realizing her own creative vision.
rationales.achievement: She "finished the Leopold mural," the owner loved it, and he is already discussing another location, marking a clear success and recognition of competence.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has "already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one," directly affirming independent choice.
rationales.security: She dismisses the "graphic design, something practical" and "safe little corporate jobs" logic, pushing against the stability-first mindset.
rationales.conformity: "I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?" shows open resistance to being pushed back into socially approved expectations.
rationales.tradition: She rejects advice coming through an older family-network figure and treats those inherited fears as something merely "parroted," rather than something to honor.

rationales.hedonism: "I'm running on fumes" shows that the workload has come at the expense of comfort and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: "Three workshops back to back this weekend" and "Good money" indicate heavy productive effort and successful output.

rationales.self_direction: Her ideal life centers on "the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me," which is a direct statement of wanting to author her own life.
rationales.hedonism: She celebrates sleeping in, a proper breakfast, music, a slow sketchbook session, and calls the Goa trip "Non-negotiable," making pleasure a clear priority.
rationales.security: She defines success as having "enough commissions to stay comfortable" and allocates money toward "rent and supplies," showing attention to a stable base.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects society's "very narrow definition" of success and argues that opting out of "the rat race" takes courage.
rationales.tradition: Her list of rejected success markers includes "marriage" and other standard life milestones, showing distance from conventional scripts.
rationales.benevolence: She frames the Goa trip as something shared with close friends, saying "We need this," which centers the wellbeing of her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: "This is what I work for" and "this is exactly where I want to be" show that she is organizing life around her own chosen priorities rather than external standards.
rationales.stimulation: The Goa trip is full of novelty and vivid experience, from a "tiny beach shack" to sunset shots and sketching boats and palm shadows.
rationales.hedonism: "Zero regrets," repeated beach dinners, and "doing things that feel good" make present enjoyment the central point of the entry.
rationales.conformity: She says "The work will always be there when I get back" and treats pleasure and relationships as "the whole point," pushing against work-first norms.
rationales.benevolence: She treasures being on the beach "with people I love" and emphasizes that time with "the people who matter" is what counts most.
